article.title: FBI and CISA Warn Russian Intelligence Has Hijacked Thousands of Signal and WhatsApp Accounts in Global Phishing Campaign
article.summary: A joint FBI-CISA advisory reveals Russian state-backed actors have compromised thousands of messaging accounts belonging to government officials, military personnel, and journalists through social engineering attacks that bypass end-to-end encryption entirely.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

The Federal Bureau of Investigation and the Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency issued a joint public service announcement on March 20 warning that cyber actors affiliated with Russian intelligence services have compromised thousands of Signal and WhatsApp accounts in a global phishing campaign. The advisory, which follows earlier warnings from Dutch and Portuguese intelligence agencies, confirms that the operation targets individuals of high intelligence value rather than the general public, and that attackers are circumventing end-to-end encryption not by breaking it but by stealing access to individual accounts through social engineering.

The campaign marks one of the most significant state-sponsored efforts to exploit the human layer of encrypted messaging platforms, raising questions about whether the security model of consumer-grade apps is sufficient for government communications.

## What We Know

According to [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/fbi-links-signal-phishing-attacks-to-russian-intelligence-services/), the FBI and CISA confirmed that Russian intelligence-linked threat actors have gained unauthorized access to thousands of messaging accounts worldwide. The agencies emphasized that the encryption built into Signal, WhatsApp, and similar platforms has not been broken; instead, the attackers rely entirely on social engineering to compromise individual accounts.

The campaign employs two primary techniques. The first exploits the linked-device feature available in both Signal and WhatsApp. Attackers send victims malicious QR codes or links that appear to be group chat invitations or contact requests. Scanning the QR code silently links the attacker's device to the victim's account, granting ongoing access to all incoming and outgoing messages without the victim's knowledge, as reported by [CyberScoop](https://cyberscoop.com/fbi-cisa-issue-psa-on-russian-intelligence-campaign-to-target-messaging-apps/).

The second technique involves direct account takeover. Attackers impersonate official platform support using names like "Signal Support" or "Signal Security Bot" and warn users of suspicious login attempts. The victim is then asked to reply with a verification code or PIN, after which the attacker registers the account on a device they control, locking the legitimate user out entirely, according to [Euronews](https://www.euronews.com/next/2026/03/24/russian-hackers-are-targeting-signal-and-whatsapp-accounts-says-uss-fbi).

Once an account is compromised, the attacker can read messages and contact lists, send messages as the victim, and use the hijacked identity to launch follow-on phishing campaigns against the victim's contacts, as detailed by [CyberScoop](https://cyberscoop.com/fbi-cisa-issue-psa-on-russian-intelligence-campaign-to-target-messaging-apps/).

The primary targets include current and former U.S. government officials, military personnel, political figures, and journalists. European targets have included diplomats and defense officials, according to [Euronews](https://www.euronews.com/next/2026/03/24/russian-hackers-are-targeting-signal-and-whatsapp-accounts-says-uss-fbi).

Dutch intelligence agencies AIVD and MIVD issued the first public warning about the campaign in early March, describing it as a "large-scale global" operation and noting that Russia targets Signal specifically because of its reputation as a secure, independent encrypted channel preferred by officials, as reported by [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/03/09/russian-government-hackers-targeting-signal-and-whatsapp-users-dutch-spies-warn/). German authorities also issued a separate warning in February. The FBI and CISA PSA, released on March 20, followed these European alerts and extended them with additional technical guidance.

Signal responded to the advisory by stating that its app support will never initiate contact via in-app messages, SMS, or social media to request verification codes, according to [Euronews](https://www.euronews.com/next/2026/03/24/russian-hackers-are-targeting-signal-and-whatsapp-accounts-says-uss-fbi).

## What We Don't Know

The FBI and CISA advisory does not identify the specific Russian intelligence units behind the campaign. No attribution to named groups such as APT28 (GRU), APT29 (SVR), or Turla (FSB) has been made publicly, leaving open the question of whether this is a coordinated effort by a single unit or a broader directive shared across Russian intelligence services.

The exact start date of the campaign remains unclear. While the public warnings began in early March, the operation may have been running for months or longer before intelligence agencies detected and disclosed it. The total number of compromised accounts is described only as "thousands," with no precise figure provided.

It is also unknown whether any classified information was accessed through compromised accounts. The use of consumer messaging apps for sensitive government communications has been a recurring policy concern, and the campaign's success may reignite debate over whether officials should be permitted to use platforms like Signal for work-related discussions.

## Analysis

The campaign illustrates a paradox at the heart of encrypted messaging security. Signal and WhatsApp offer some of the strongest end-to-end encryption available in consumer software, yet the attack surface extends well beyond cryptographic protocols. By targeting the account authentication layer rather than the encryption itself, Russian intelligence has demonstrated that the weakest link in secure communications remains the user.

The linked-device technique is particularly effective because it does not trigger the kind of warnings most users associate with account compromise. Unlike a full account takeover, which locks the original user out and may be noticed immediately, a silently linked device can monitor conversations indefinitely while the victim continues using the app normally.

The coordinated disclosure by Dutch, German, and American intelligence agencies suggests the campaign reached a scale that warranted a rare multi-national public warning. For organizations that rely on encrypted messaging for sensitive communications, the advisory is a reminder that encryption alone does not constitute a security strategy. The FBI and CISA recommend that users treat unsolicited messages with suspicion, never share verification codes or PINs, block and report unknown contacts, regularly audit linked devices, and enable all available security features including registration lock PINs and disappearing messages.
